meta.title: SG-SCD-6: Embedded Analytics, Modeling, and Governed Input Workflows

questions.stem: An exam scenario describes the following situation. what does Sigma version history help project teams do in this scenario?

options.text: Rotate service-account credentials
options.explanation: is correct here because version history supports review and rollback of workbook changes. **B** is incorrect because it does not materialize data. **C** is incorrect because credential rotation is an admin task. **D** is incorrect because embed tokens are separate. [Workbooks overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/workbooks-overview)

options.text: Review and restore prior workbook states
Build embed tokens
Materialize warehouse tables automatically

questions.stem: A candidate reviews a workbook requirement. A planning workflow needs business users to enter assumptions directly in Sigma. Which feature supports that requirement?

options.text: A context filter
A dashboard subscription only
A warehouse resize policy
An input table
options.explanation: An input table is correct because Sigma input tables are designed for user-entered data and planning-style workflows. Filters, subscriptions, and warehouse sizing do not capture governed entered values.

questions.stem: You need to choose the most appropriate action. what should be rotated periodically for connections that use service-account credentials?

options.text: The credentials or secrets for the service account
options.explanation: is correct here because credential rotation is a standard operational security practice. **B** is incorrect because tabs, rows, and controls are unrelated to secret hygiene. **C** is incorrect because not applicable. [Permissions and sharing](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/share-content-and-manage-permissions)

options.text: Input-table rows
Date controls
Workbook tabs

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to avoid a common mistake. A product team wants embedded users to inherit region-based row restrictions from the application context. What embed design is appropriate?

options.text: Export all data and filter it in JavaScript only
Share one generic admin account with every customer
Disable Sigma authentication checks
Pass trusted user attributes or claims in the embed configuration
options.explanation: Trusted user attributes or claims are correct because embedded experiences commonly personalize access with signed context. Client-side-only filtering, shared admin accounts, and disabled authentication are insecure.

questions.stem: An analyst needs the most appropriate next step. To keep records present in each source on the join key and drop nonmatches, which join type fits?

options.text: No modeling step at all
Inner join
options.explanation: An inner join is correct because it retains rows that match on the join key in both tables. Cross joins, duplication, and skipping modeling do not satisfy the matching-keys requirement.

options.text: Cross join
Full duplication without a join

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to avoid a common mistake. Users need extra detail on hover while keeping the chart layout unchanged. What capability should be used?

options.text: Tooltips on the visualization
options.explanation: Tooltips are correct because they provide additional context on hover without changing the displayed grain. JWT claims, duplication, and warehouse failover do not address chart hover details.

options.text: JWT team claims
Workbook duplication
Warehouse failover

questions.stem: A candidate reviews a workbook requirement. A developer can open a workbook but cannot change its dataset. What permission gap is the most likely cause?

options.text: Input tables always override object permissions
Workbook controls prevent dataset editing
They have view access but not edit permission on the relevant content or source object
options.explanation: A view-versus-edit permission gap is correct because Sigma permissions can allow access without granting modification rights. Organization-admin requirements, input tables, and controls are not the cause.

options.text: Sigma requires all viewers to be organization admins

questions.stem: An analytics team is reviewing its next step. a developer wants to troubleshoot why a workbook calculation is expensive. What is the most useful first step?

options.text: Disable permissions checks
options.explanation: is correct here because performance troubleshooting starts by understanding the query shape and reducing expensive operations. **B** is incorrect because API token rotation is unrelated. **C** is incorrect because permissions are not the root cause of query complexity. **D** is incorrect because moving workspaces alone rarely fixes the query plan. [Materialization](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/materialization-overview)

options.text: Rotate every API token
Inspect the generated query path and simplify filters, joins, or calculations that block efficient pushdown
Publish the workbook to a different workspace only

questions.stem: You need to choose the most appropriate action. which TWO workbook design choices most appropriate support a polished analyst experience? (Select TWO)

options.text: Avoid datasets so each page starts from scratch
options.explanation: are correct because clear organization and purposeful controls improve usability and maintainability. **C** is incorrect because permanently hiding needed filters hurts usability. **D** is incorrect because avoiding datasets reduces reuse and consistency. [Workbooks overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/workbooks-overview)

options.text: Organize related pages and reusable navigation clearly
options.explanation: are correct because clear organization and purposeful controls improve usability and maintainability. **C** is incorrect because permanently hiding needed filters hurts usability. **D** is incorrect because avoiding datasets reduces reuse and consistency. [Workbooks overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/workbooks-overview)

options.text: Hide all filters permanently even when needed
Use controls that target relevant elements

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to avoid a common mistake. A join condition is missing and result sets explode. What is the main danger?

options.text: It prevents users from exporting CSV results
It can create a Cartesian product that dramatically increases rows and query cost
options.explanation: A Cartesian-product explosion is correct because cross joins multiply row counts and often create expensive warehouse work. Controls, source deletion, and export behavior are unrelated.

options.text: It automatically disables workbook controls
It permanently deletes the source dataset

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. which approach is used for client-side embedded Sigma analytics?

options.text: Materialize every workbook page to a Cloud Storage bucket before embedding
options.explanation: Sigma embedding is based on generating a signed URL (or JWT-based token) server-side with appropriate claims such as user email, team, and user attributes, then rendering it in an iframe within the host application.

options.text: Export the workbook to a static HTML file and host it on the customer's CDN
options.explanation: Sigma workbooks are dynamic, live-querying documents; they cannot be meaningfully exported to static HTML while retaining interactive filtering and real-time data.

options.text: Generate a signed embed URL or JWT on the application server and load it in an iframe
options.explanation: Materialization is an optional performance optimization for caching expensive queries; it is not required before embedding and does not produce an embeddable artifact.

options.text: Copy the workbook data into the embedding application's own database first
options.explanation: Sigma always queries the connected warehouse at query time; data is not copied into the embedding application's database.

questions.stem: An analyst needs the most appropriate next step. One team needs faster access to a frequently reused result set. Which option is most relevant?

options.text: Replacing all filters with text boxes
Disabling dataset governance
Materialization of the expensive result set
options.explanation: Materialization is correct because it can persist expensive query results for faster reuse. The other choices either increase complexity or weaken governance without solving repeated compute.

options.text: Adding more worksheets to the workbook

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to avoid a common mistake. Administrators want users to see only the content they need. What permission strategy fits best?

options.text: Replace permissions with input table writeback rules
Grant access only to the specific folders or workbooks each audience needs
options.explanation: Granular sharing is correct because it limits access to only the required content. Broad edit access, disabled authentication, and writeback settings do not provide least-privilege content control.

options.text: Make all workspaces editable by every viewer
Turn off authentication for internal users

questions.stem: A candidate reviews a workbook requirement. A team needs a single current value with perhaps a small comparison. What is the most suitable presentation?

options.text: An embed token editor
A warehouse permission matrix
A KPI or single-value visualization
options.explanation: A KPI-style single-value visual is correct because it communicates one important metric quickly. The other options are not designed to present a simple headline measure.

options.text: A cross join configuration table

questions.stem: One team is designing a new solution. Two datasets must be combined while preserving only matching business keys. Which join type is usually appropriate?

options.text: No modeling step at all
Inner join
options.explanation: An inner join is correct because it retains rows that match on the join key in both tables. Cross joins, duplication, and skipping modeling do not satisfy the matching-keys requirement.

options.text: Cross join
Full duplication without a join

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to avoid a common mistake. A Sigma developer wants reusable curated logic for many workbooks. What should they publish?

options.text: A dataset that centralizes the modeled logic
options.explanation: A dataset is correct because it lets teams publish reusable modeled data for many workbooks. Screenshots, bookmarks, and notes do not create governed reusable logic.

options.text: A screenshot of the current workbook
A browser bookmark collection
A personal note attached to a chart

questions.stem: You need to choose the most appropriate action. a user should be able to explore and build ad hoc analyses but not administer the entire organization. Which Sigma role is the closest fit?

options.text: Viewer, because Viewers can explore workbooks and create new ones in their personal space
options.explanation: The Viewer role is focused on consuming published content; Viewers typically cannot create or edit workbooks, which makes it too restrictive for ad hoc analysis.

options.text: There is no built-in role for this; a custom role must be created via the API
options.explanation: The Creator and Explorer account types allow users to build analyses, create workbooks, and explore data without having the organization-wide administrative capabilities of the Admin role.

options.text: Creator or Explorer, because these roles support analysis and content authoring without organization-wide administration
options.explanation: Admin is the most privileged role, with access to organization settings, connections, and user management — far broader than what an analyst needs.

options.text: Admin, because ad hoc analysis requires elevated data access permissions
options.explanation: Sigma provides built-in account types that cover this use case; a custom API-created role is not required.

questions.stem: During exam preparation, you evaluate this scenario. Administrators want to reduce repeated compute for a costly query path. Which feature should they evaluate?

options.text: Replacing all filters with text boxes
Disabling dataset governance
Materialization of the expensive result set
options.explanation: Materialization is correct because it can persist expensive query results for faster reuse. The other choices either increase complexity or weaken governance without solving repeated compute.

options.text: Adding more worksheets to the workbook

questions.stem: An analyst needs the most appropriate next step. A company wants least-privilege access to analytics content. Which sharing approach is safest?

options.text: Replace permissions with input table writeback rules
Grant access only to the specific folders or workbooks each audience needs
options.explanation: Granular sharing is correct because it limits access to only the required content. Broad edit access, disabled authentication, and writeback settings do not provide least-privilege content control.

options.text: Make all workspaces editable by every viewer
Turn off authentication for internal users

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. why might a project team publish a Sigma dataset in this scenario?

options.text: To bypass the warehouse completely
options.explanation: is correct here because published datasets support reuse and governance across workbooks. **B** is incorrect because Sigma still queries the warehouse. **C** is incorrect because joins remain available. **D** is incorrect because datasets are not the same as APIs. [Datasets overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/datasets)

options.text: To disable joins
To create a reusable governed semantic layer for many workbooks
To convert every workbook into an API

questions.stem: During exam preparation, you evaluate this scenario. An application wants embedded Sigma analytics for signed-in users. What should the host application generate server-side?

options.text: A copy of all workbook data in the app database
A browser extension that bypasses Sigma authentication
A signed embed URL or JWT with the required claims
options.explanation: A signed embed URL or JWT is correct because Sigma embedding relies on server-generated trusted context. Static exports, data copying, and authentication bypasses are not the intended pattern.

options.text: A static HTML export of the workbook

questions.stem: A candidate reviews a workbook requirement. One team wants supplemental context without adding new visible columns to the chart. Which feature is best?

options.text: Tooltips on the visualization
options.explanation: Tooltips are correct because they provide additional context on hover without changing the displayed grain. JWT claims, duplication, and warehouse failover do not address chart hover details.

options.text: JWT team claims
Workbook duplication
Warehouse failover

questions.stem: An exam scenario describes the following situation. why is granular sharing generally safer than granting broad workspace access to everyone?

options.text: It disables embedding risks
options.explanation: is correct here because granular sharing supports least-privilege access. **B** is incorrect because cost is a separate concern. **C** is incorrect because sharing does not automatically replace data filtering. **D** is incorrect because embedding still requires secure design. [Permissions and sharing](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/share-content-and-manage-permissions)

options.text: It limits access to only the content a user actually needs
It guarantees lower warehouse cost
It replaces row-level filtering automatically

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to solve the following problem. which TWO statements about embedding Sigma are accurate? (Select TWO)

options.text: Embed parameters can pass dynamic context such as tenant filters
options.explanation: are correct because signed embed authorization and dynamic parameters are common embedded analytics patterns in Sigma. **C** is incorrect because materialization is optional and performance-related, not mandatory for embedding. **D** is incorrect because embedding still requires correct permission and security design. [Embed analytics with Sigma](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/embed-analytics-overview)

options.text: Embedding removes the need for permission design
options.explanation: are correct because signed embed authorization and dynamic parameters are common embedded analytics patterns in Sigma. **C** is incorrect because materialization is optional and performance-related, not mandatory for embedding. **D** is incorrect because embedding still requires correct permission and security design. [Embed analytics with Sigma](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/embed-analytics-overview)

options.text: Signed JWTs can authorize embedded access
Embedding requires materialization of every workbook

questions.stem: A candidate reviews a workbook requirement. A workbook needs one selector to drive several charts at the same time. Which Sigma feature should the developer configure?

options.text: A control with targets mapped to the relevant elements
options.explanation: A control with targets is correct because Sigma controls can drive one or more workbook elements for coordinated filtering. Warehouse roles, materialization schedules, and embed claims solve different problems.

options.text: A warehouse role assigned to each chart
A materialization schedule on every worksheet
A JWT embed claim that rewrites filter logic

questions.stem: During exam preparation, you evaluate this scenario. A page contains several KPI tiles and charts that should react to the same user input. What should be added?

options.text: A control with targets mapped to the relevant elements
options.explanation: A control with targets is correct because Sigma controls can drive one or more workbook elements for coordinated filtering. Warehouse roles, materialization schedules, and embed claims solve different problems.

options.text: A warehouse role assigned to each chart
A materialization schedule on every worksheet
A JWT embed claim that rewrites filter logic

questions.stem: An analytics team is reviewing its next step. what is the primary purpose of a Sigma workbook?

options.text: A low-level warehouse driver
options.explanation: is correct here because a Sigma workbook is the core collaborative surface for analysis and presentation. **B** is incorrect because warehouse drivers connect to data sources but are not the analytical document. **C** is incorrect because Sigma does not replace underlying warehouse storage. **D** is incorrect because it is not an OS package. [Workbooks overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/workbooks-overview)

options.text: A replacement for warehouse storage
An operating-system package
A collaborative analytical document containing pages and data elements

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. which Sigma feature supports manual business-user writeback to the connected warehouse?

options.text: Input tables
options.explanation: Input tables are designed specifically for governed manual data entry and writeback use cases, allowing business users to type values that are persisted back to the warehouse.

options.text: Version-tagged workbook templates
options.explanation: Workbook controls — such as dropdowns or date pickers — filter or parameterize the analytical view; they do not write new records to the warehouse.

options.text: Workbook controls with a list-value source
options.explanation: Version tags track content milestones for rollback and audit, not for data writeback.

options.text: Scheduled materialization jobs
options.explanation: Materialization schedules cache existing query results as warehouse tables; they do not accept user-entered data.

questions.stem: During exam preparation, you evaluate this scenario. A workbook editor reports that dataset changes are blocked even though the workbook opens normally. What is the best explanation?

options.text: Input tables always override object permissions
Workbook controls prevent dataset editing
They have view access but not edit permission on the relevant content or source object
options.explanation: A view-versus-edit permission gap is correct because Sigma permissions can allow access without granting modification rights. Organization-admin requirements, input tables, and controls are not the cause.

options.text: Sigma requires all viewers to be organization admins

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to solve the following problem. which role is usually most restricted in Sigma?

options.text: Admin
options.explanation: Creators have broader capabilities than Viewers, including the ability to build and publish workbooks.

options.text: Viewer
options.explanation: Explorers can also explore and interact with data more deeply than Viewers, such as editing in their personal Explore space.

options.text: Explorer
options.explanation: The Viewer account type provides the most restricted access — Viewers can consume published workbooks and interact with controls but cannot create, edit, or save workbooks, making it the least privileged of the common user-facing account types.

options.text: Creator
options.explanation: Admin is the most privileged account type, with full control over organization settings, connections, and user management.

questions.stem: A candidate reviews a workbook requirement. Why would a developer prefer Sigma input tables over ad hoc spreadsheet uploads for collaborative planning?

options.text: They automatically bypass warehouse permissions
They eliminate the need for any data model
They convert every value into a visualization
They support structured data entry within a governed workflow
options.explanation: Structured governed data entry is correct because input tables support controlled user submissions in the analytics workflow. They do not bypass permissions, remove modeling, or turn all values into charts.

questions.stem: An analyst needs the most appropriate next step. Why should a Sigma developer avoid accidental cross joins in warehouse-backed analytics?

options.text: It prevents users from exporting CSV results
It can create a Cartesian product that dramatically increases rows and query cost
options.explanation: A Cartesian-product explosion is correct because cross joins multiply row counts and often create expensive warehouse work. Controls, source deletion, and export behavior are unrelated.

options.text: It automatically disables workbook controls
It permanently deletes the source dataset

questions.stem: A team is designing a new solution. Users must type forecast adjustments into a governed table from the workbook experience. What should the developer use?

options.text: A context filter
A dashboard subscription only
A warehouse resize policy
An input table
options.explanation: An input table is correct because Sigma input tables are designed for user-entered data and planning-style workflows. Filters, subscriptions, and warehouse sizing do not capture governed entered values.

questions.stem: An analyst needs the most appropriate next step. One team wants users to adjust assumptions but still audit who changed what. Why are governed input-table workflows useful?

options.text: They automatically bypass warehouse permissions
They eliminate the need for any data model
They convert every value into a visualization
They support structured data entry within a governed workflow
options.explanation: Structured governed data entry is correct because input tables support controlled user submissions in the analytics workflow. They do not bypass permissions, remove modeling, or turn all values into charts.

questions.stem: An analytics team is reviewing its next step. which function family is best for replacing nulls with fallback values?

options.text: Workspace functions
options.explanation: is correct here because functions like `Coalesce` and `IsNull` handle null-related logic. **B** is incorrect because the other choices are not Sigma calculation families. **C** is incorrect because not applicable. [Workbooks overview](https://help.sigmacomputing.com/docs/workbooks-overview)

options.text: JWT expiry functions
Embedding functions
Conditional functions such as `Coalesce` or `IsNull`

questions.stem: One team is designing a new solution. For a homepage summary, which element best communicates one important number quickly?

options.text: An embed token editor
A warehouse permission matrix
A KPI or single-value visualization
options.explanation: A KPI-style single-value visual is correct because it communicates one important metric quickly. The other options are not designed to present a simple headline measure.

options.text: A cross join configuration table

questions.stem: One team is designing a new solution. Users must type forecast adjustments into a governed table from the workbook experience. What should the developer use?

options.text: A context filter
A dashboard subscription only
A warehouse resize policy
An input table
options.explanation: An input table is correct because Sigma input tables are designed for user-entered data and planning-style workflows. Filters, subscriptions, and warehouse sizing do not capture governed entered values.

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to avoid a common mistake. For secure embedding, what should be produced before the iframe loads the Sigma content?

options.text: A copy of all workbook data in the app database
A browser extension that bypasses Sigma authentication
A signed embed URL or JWT with the required claims
options.explanation: A signed embed URL or JWT is correct because Sigma embedding relies on server-generated trusted context. Static exports, data copying, and authentication bypasses are not the intended pattern.

options.text: A static HTML export of the workbook

questions.stem: An analytics team is reviewing its next step. why should a Sigma developer avoid unnecessary cross-joins in workbook modeling?

options.text: Cross-joins multiply row counts between tables, producing extremely large intermediate result sets and expensive warehouse queries
options.explanation: Sigma's visual join builder supports multiple join types; while cross-joins may require specific configuration, they are not entirely unsupported in Sigma.

options.text: Cross-joins disable workbook controls from filtering the joined elements
options.explanation: The presence of a cross-join does not prevent materialization from being configured; materialization can persist any query result, including expensive ones.

options.text: Cross-joins are not supported in Sigma's visual join builder and must be written in raw SQL
options.explanation: Workbook controls and filters operate on the output of the query regardless of join type; cross-joins do not disable control filtering.

options.text: Cross-joins prevent materialization from being scheduled on the affected workbook
options.explanation: A cross-join produces the Cartesian product of two tables — a table with M rows joined to one with N rows produces M × N output rows. For large tables this creates massive intermediate result sets that consume significant warehouse compute, memory, and cost, often unintentionally.

questions.stem: One team is designing a new solution. A large join runs before filters and the workbook becomes slow. What is the best first optimization?

options.text: Duplicate the workbook into another folder
Remove all controls from the page
Switch every table to manual refresh
Push selective filters earlier so fewer rows enter the join
options.explanation: Applying selective filters earlier is correct because reducing join input volume is a primary way to lower query cost and latency. The other choices do not address the root query-shape problem.

questions.stem: During exam preparation, you evaluate this scenario. A scenario requires writeback-style user entry for planning values. Which Sigma capability fits best?

options.text: A context filter
A dashboard subscription only
A warehouse resize policy
An input table
options.explanation: An input table is correct because Sigma input tables are designed for user-entered data and planning-style workflows. Filters, subscriptions, and warehouse sizing do not capture governed entered values.

questions.stem: A team is designing a new solution. Users must change one date range and have multiple elements respond together. Which feature handles that shared interaction?

options.text: A control with targets mapped to the relevant elements
options.explanation: A control with targets is correct because Sigma controls can drive one or more workbook elements for coordinated filtering. Warehouse roles, materialization schedules, and embed claims solve different problems.

options.text: A warehouse role assigned to each chart
A materialization schedule on every worksheet
A JWT embed claim that rewrites filter logic

questions.stem: A team is designing a new solution. A team wants one governed semantic layer for repeat reporting. What should be created?

options.text: A dataset that centralizes the modeled logic
options.explanation: A dataset is correct because it lets teams publish reusable modeled data for many workbooks. Screenshots, bookmarks, and notes do not create governed reusable logic.

options.text: A screenshot of the current workbook
A browser bookmark collection
A personal note attached to a chart

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to avoid a common mistake. Planning users need writeback-like updates with accountability. What is the main benefit of using the platform feature for this?

options.text: They automatically bypass warehouse permissions
They eliminate the need for any data model
They convert every value into a visualization
They support structured data entry within a governed workflow
options.explanation: Structured governed data entry is correct because input tables support controlled user submissions in the analytics workflow. They do not bypass permissions, remove modeling, or turn all values into charts.

questions.stem: One team is designing a new solution. An external app needs per-user context passed into embedded Sigma content. What should the implementer rely on?

options.text: Export all data and filter it in JavaScript only
Share one generic admin account with every customer
Disable Sigma authentication checks
Pass trusted user attributes or claims in the embed configuration
options.explanation: Trusted user attributes or claims are correct because embedded experiences commonly personalize access with signed context. Client-side-only filtering, shared admin accounts, and disabled authentication are insecure.
